title: What Is an Expense Ratio? A Beginner's Guide for 2026
excerpt: Learn what an expense ratio is, how fund fees work, and how to compare ETFs and mutual funds without letting costs quietly eat into your returns.
content:
If you are buying an ETF or mutual fund, one of the most important numbers to check is the expense ratio. It looks small, often a fraction of 1%, but it can quietly reduce your returns year after year.

That is why beginners should learn this concept early. A fund with a low expense ratio does not guarantee great performance, but a high expense ratio guarantees that more of your money goes to fees instead of staying invested.

In this guide, you will learn what an expense ratio is, how it works, what counts as a good expense ratio in 2026, and how to compare funds more confidently. If you are still learning the basics, start with our guides on [index funds for beginners](/blog/index-funds-for-beginners), [how to open a brokerage account](/blog/how-to-open-brokerage-account-beginners-guide), and [what is a three-fund portfolio](/blog/what-is-a-three-fund-portfolio).

## What is an expense ratio?

An expense ratio is the annual percentage a fund charges to cover the cost of running the fund. You will usually see it on ETFs, mutual funds, index funds, and target date funds.

Those costs may include things like:

- Portfolio management
- Administration
- Recordkeeping
- Legal and accounting expenses
- Marketing and distribution fees

If a fund has a 0.20% expense ratio, that means you pay about $2 per year for every $1,000 invested. If a fund has a 1.00% expense ratio, that is about $10 per year for every $1,000 invested.

The percentage may look tiny, but fees compound over time just like returns do.

## How does an expense ratio work?

An expense ratio is not usually billed to you as a separate charge. Instead, it is deducted from the fund's assets, which means it quietly lowers your return.

In practical terms, you will not log in and see a line item that says, fee paid today. The cost is built into the fund's performance. That is one reason many beginners overlook it.

For example, imagine two similar funds both earn the same gross return before fees. If one charges 0.05% and the other charges 0.75%, the lower-cost fund lets you keep more of the gain. Over one year the difference may feel small. Over 10, 20, or 30 years, it can become meaningful.

## Why expense ratios matter so much

Expense ratios matter because they are one of the few investing costs you can control before you buy.

You cannot control what the market does next month. You cannot control interest rates or headlines. But you can compare funds and avoid paying more than necessary for a similar product.

That matters even more for long-term investors. If you are investing for retirement, every extra bit lost to fees is money that is no longer compounding for you.

A simple way to think about it is this: lower costs leave more of your return in your account.

## What does a 0.50% expense ratio actually cost?

Here is a simple way to translate percentages into dollars:

- 0.03% = about $0.30 per year for every $1,000 invested
- 0.10% = about $1 per year for every $1,000 invested
- 0.50% = about $5 per year for every $1,000 invested
- 1.00% = about $10 per year for every $1,000 invested

Now scale that up:

- On $10,000 invested, a 1.00% expense ratio is about $100 per year
- On $100,000 invested, a 1.00% expense ratio is about $1,000 per year

That does not mean every higher-cost fund is automatically bad. Some specialized or actively managed funds charge more for a reason. But it does mean you should know what you are paying and why.

## What is included in an expense ratio?

An expense ratio generally covers a fund's ongoing operating expenses. That can include:

- Management fees
- Administrative costs
- Custodial and accounting services
- Distribution or marketing fees in some mutual funds
- Other operating costs

It does not always include every cost an investor might face. For example, trading commissions, taxes, bid-ask spreads on ETFs, or some transaction costs inside the fund may not be fully reflected in the published expense ratio.

That is why expense ratio is important, but it is not the only cost worth checking.

## Gross expense ratio vs net expense ratio

When researching a fund, you may see two numbers:

### Gross expense ratio

This is the fund's total operating expense before temporary fee waivers or reimbursements.

### Net expense ratio

This is what investors are effectively paying after fee waivers or reimbursements are applied.

For beginners, the net expense ratio is often the more useful number because it is closer to your current real-world cost. Still, fee waivers can expire, so it is smart to notice when a fund's lower net expense ratio depends on a temporary subsidy.

## What is a good expense ratio in 2026?

A good expense ratio depends on the kind of fund you are comparing. The right question is not, is this fee low in absolute terms? The better question is, is this fee reasonable for this category and strategy?

In general:

- Broad index funds and plain vanilla ETFs often have very low expense ratios
- Actively managed funds usually charge more
- Niche, international, sector, or specialty funds may also cost more

According to recent industry data, low-cost index ETFs and index mutual funds often fall far below the expense ratios charged by actively managed funds. That gap is one reason index investing remains popular with beginners and long-term investors.

As a rough rule of thumb:

- Under 0.10% is very low for a broad index fund
- Around 0.10% to 0.25% can still be reasonable for many index funds and ETFs
- Around 0.40% to 0.75% deserves closer comparison, especially for a basic diversified fund
- Above 1.00% is often expensive, unless there is a very specific reason you want that fund

These are not hard laws. They are a quick screening tool.

## Expense ratio vs management fee

People often use these terms as if they mean the same thing, but they are not always identical.

A management fee is the amount paid to the investment manager for running the fund. The expense ratio is broader. It may include the management fee plus other operating expenses.

So if you only compare management fees, you may miss part of the total ongoing cost.

## Expense ratio vs sales load

Another common point of confusion is the difference between an expense ratio and a sales load.

- An expense ratio is an ongoing annual cost
- A sales load is a one-time commission or sales charge some mutual funds impose when you buy or sell

A fund can have one, both, or neither. Many beginner-friendly brokerages now offer plenty of no-load funds, but it is still worth checking the prospectus or fund details page.

## Are lower expense ratios always better?

Usually lower is better when you are comparing funds that do the same basic job. But lower is not the only thing that matters.

You should also look at:

- What index or strategy the fund follows
- How diversified it is
- Whether it fits your goal and time horizon
- Tax efficiency, if you are investing in a taxable account
- Tracking error, liquidity, and fund size

For example, a fund with a 0.03% expense ratio that does not match your investing goal is not automatically better than a fund with a 0.12% expense ratio that gives you the exposure you actually need.

Still, when two funds are very similar, the lower-cost option often has the edge.

## Why actively managed funds usually cost more

Actively managed funds have teams making ongoing decisions about what to buy, what to sell, and how to position the portfolio. That research and trading infrastructure costs money.

Index funds work differently. They usually aim to track a benchmark rather than beat it. Because the strategy is more rules-based, operating costs can be lower.

That is why many beginners start with low-cost index funds or ETFs. They are simple, diversified, and often cheaper to hold long term.

## How to find a fund's expense ratio

You can usually find it in a few places:

- The fund's prospectus
- Your brokerage's fund research page
- The ETF or mutual fund provider's website
- Retirement plan fund details, if you are investing through a 401(k)

Look for wording like expense ratio, net expense ratio, annual operating expenses, or fund fees and expenses.

If you are comparing options in a workplace retirement plan, make sure you compare similar funds. A target date fund should not be judged against a plain S&P 500 index fund as if they are interchangeable products.

## A simple example of fee drag over time

Imagine you have two similar funds and invest $25,000. Both earn the same return before fees. One fund charges 0.15% and the other charges 0.40%.

At first, the difference may not feel dramatic. But over many years, the higher-cost fund keeps taking a larger bite out of your balance. That is the key idea behind fee drag. Small percentages can create large dollar differences when your balance grows and your timeline is long.

This is one reason cost-conscious investors pay attention to expense ratios early, not just once their portfolio becomes large.

## When a higher expense ratio might be acceptable

There are cases where paying more can be reasonable:

- You want a very specific niche exposure
- Your 401(k) has limited choices
- You prefer an all-in-one target date fund for convenience
- A specialty bond or international fund naturally has higher operating costs

The point is not to chase the lowest fee at all costs. The point is to avoid paying a premium for a fund that does not give you extra value.

## Common mistakes beginners make

### Ignoring fees completely

Many new investors focus only on past returns. That can be a mistake because fees are certain, while future outperformance is not.

### Comparing unlike funds

A U.S. total market index fund and an emerging markets specialty fund are different products. Comparing only the expense ratio can be misleading.

### Assuming expensive means better

In investing, higher fees do not automatically mean higher quality. Sometimes they just mean higher fees.

### Missing temporary waivers

A low net expense ratio can look great today, but if the waiver expires, the fund may become more expensive later.

### Forgetting retirement-plan costs

A fund's expense ratio is only part of the picture. Some 401(k) plans also layer on administrative fees.

## How to use expense ratios when choosing funds

A practical approach is:

1. Start with the kind of fund you actually need
2. Compare similar funds in the same category
3. Check the net expense ratio
4. Review diversification, strategy, and tracking quality
5. Choose the lower-cost option when all else is reasonably similar

If you are building a simple portfolio, this process pairs well with low-cost broad funds. That is also why expense ratios matter so much in strategies like a [three-fund portfolio](/blog/what-is-a-three-fund-portfolio).

## Frequently asked questions

### What is an expense ratio in simple terms?

It is the annual percentage a fund charges to cover operating costs. The fee is deducted from the fund, so it lowers your return without showing up as a separate bill.

### Is a 0.75% expense ratio high?

It can be high for a plain index fund or broad ETF. It may be more normal for an actively managed or specialized fund. The key is comparing it with similar funds.

### Do you pay expense ratios directly?

Usually no. The cost is taken out of the fund's assets and reflected in performance.

### What is the difference between gross and net expense ratio?

Gross is the total cost before waivers. Net is the current effective cost after waivers or reimbursements.

### Should beginners care about expense ratios?

Yes. Expense ratios are one of the easiest fund costs to compare before investing, and they can have a meaningful effect over time.

## Sources

- Investor.gov, Expense Ratio: https://www.investor.gov/introduction-investing/investing-basics/glossary/expense-ratio
- Investor.gov, Mutual Fund and ETF Fees and Expenses: https://www.investor.gov/introduction-investing/general-resources/news-alerts/alerts-bulletins/investor-bulletins/mutual-fund-and-etf-fees-and-expenses-investor-bulletin
- Vanguard, Expense ratios: What they are and why they matter: https://investor.vanguard.com/investor-resources-education/education/expense-ratio
- Fidelity, What is an expense ratio?: https://www.fidelity.com/learning-center/smart-money/expense-ratio
- Investment Company Institute, US Equity Fund Fees Continue to Decline Amid Rising Investor Demand for Lower-Cost Options: https://www.ici.org/news-release/25-news-fund-fees-decline

## The bottom line

An expense ratio is a small percentage with a big long-term effect. It tells you how much a fund costs to run each year, and that cost comes out of your return.

For beginners, the best move is usually simple: compare similar funds, understand the net expense ratio, and avoid paying more than necessary for broad market exposure. You do not need to obsess over every basis point, but you should absolutely know what your fund is charging before you buy.
